pattern: Splitting the path into unit steps shows every step from position i earns whatever value started that jump, and starting a jump as late as possible while still using the biggest value seen so far is always at least as good, so the answer is the running prefix maximum summed across every step.
hints: Instead of thinking about which indices you land on, think about each unit gap between i and i+1: it always gets 'charged' the value of nums at the start of whichever jump currently covers it.
You are never worse off starting a jump from the largest value you have seen so far rather than an earlier, smaller one, so track a running maximum as you scan left to right.
Sum, for every index from 0 to n-2, the maximum of nums[0..i]; that total is the best achievable score, no explicit jumping required.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two-hop path beats the direct jump
statement example, direct jump to the end is best
two elements, the only possible move
strictly increasing values, tempting to jump early instead of riding the max
strictly decreasing values, the first element dominates every step

stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: spike pattern: one huge value near the start then small values
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 5) for _ in range(n)]
    nums[1] = 100000
    return [nums]
